back: Deployment is the phase where software is made available for use in a production environment, transitioning from development to live usage.
Automated deployment uses tools and scripts to automatically package, configure, and deploy applications to various environments without manual intervention.
CI (Continuous Integration) automatically merges code changes, while CD (Continuous Delivery/Deployment) automates the release process to production.
A deployment strategy with two identical production environments (blue = active, green = standby) enabling instant rollback by switching traffic.
A gradual rollout strategy where new version is released to a small subset of users first, then progressively expanded.
A deployment strategy that incrementally updates instances, replacing old versions with new ones one at a time or in small batches.
Managing infrastructure through code/version-controlled files, enabling consistent and repeatable deployments using tools like Terraform, Ansible.
The practice of maintaining consistent configuration across all environments through automated tools like Puppet, Chef, Ansible.
An automated workflow that moves code from version control through building, testing, and deploying to production environments.
Infrastructure where servers are never modified after deployment; instead, they are replaced with new configured instances for updates.
Deployment strategy that allows updating applications without service interruption, ensuring continuous availability to users.
The process of reverting to a previous stable version when issues are detected in the new deployment.
Packaging application with its dependencies in isolated containers (Docker) ensuring consistent behavior across environments.
Automating deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications using tools like Kubernetes, Docker Swarm.
Using Git repositories as the single source of truth for infrastructure and application configurations, enabling version-controlled deployments.
